title: My Melbourne lunch with Modi
gist: A first-person account of Narendra Modi's 9-10 July 2026 Melbourne visit argues the stadium pageantry and choreographed lunch functioned as a distraction from scrutiny, while the substantive outcome was a plan to operationalise Australian uranium exports to India alongside deals across sport, diaspora and trade.

insights.claim: The large stadium events that accompany Narendra Modi's overseas visits generate media coverage through colour and crowds, but also function as a distraction that makes it easier to avoid probing the substance of the visit's deals.
insights.speaker: Aarti Betigeri
insights.so_what: A lens for discounting the political narrative around leader-visit spectacles and instead tracking the actual agreements signed.
insights.quote: the colour, movement and crowds ensure media coverage, and also serve as a useful distraction
insights.locator: The stadium events synonymous with Modi's visits

insights.claim: At the MCG lunch honouring Modi, there was no speech and no press question opportunity; Modi and Albanese spoke privately for about 45 minutes before leaving without any public remarks.
insights.speaker: Aarti Betigeri
insights.so_what: Confirms Modi's well-known avoidance of open press engagement extends even to high-profile bilateral hospitality settings.
insights.locator: There was no sign of a speech

insights.claim: Not all Indian-Australian migrants share the enthusiasm of Modi's diaspora rally attendees; one skeptical Uber driver described India's economy as being 'down, a hell of a lot down,' citing corruption, youth unemployment, rising drug use and a leaked national medical entrance exam (NEET) that led to at least a dozen suicides.
insights.speaker: Aarti Betigeri
insights.so_what: A ground-level counter-narrative to headline diaspora enthusiasm — useful as a check on India growth-story sentiment among its own emigrant base.
insights.quote: The economy is down, a hell of a lot down.
insights.locator: In the five-kilometre journey from my place to the MCG

insights.claim: The centrepiece outcome of Modi's Melbourne visit was advancing practical plans for Australian uranium exports to India, building on the 2014-announced nuclear trade deal, made possible after India updated its nuclear policy via the 2025 SHANTI Act.
insights.speaker: Aarti Betigeri
insights.so_what: Confirms the uranium supply arrangement as the substantive deliverable of the visit, not the diplomatic theatre around it.
insights.quote: This was no hollow and easily forgettable announceable, but rather one that starts the practical plans for sending Australian uranium to India.
insights.locator: Understandably, it had been a busy 24 hours

insights.claim: The AFL is working to expand Australian rules football in India, aiming to grow the player base from roughly 10,000 to 100,000, alongside plans for a 'Little India' precinct in Melbourne's Docklands.
insights.speaker: Aarti Betigeri
insights.so_what: Signals soft-power and cultural-market expansion running in parallel with the harder energy and trade deals.
insights.locator: the AFL is working to galvanise more players

insights.claim: In Tarneit, western Melbourne, roughly a quarter of the population is now Indian-born, illustrating the scale and concentration of India's diaspora growth in Australia.
insights.speaker: Aarti Betigeri
insights.so_what: A concrete demographic marker for the pace of India-linked migration reshaping specific Australian suburbs, relevant to housing, services and consumer-market planning.
insights.locator: I talked to people working at the coalface of India's diaspora

insights.claim: Punjabi truck drivers in Australia are experiencing widespread racism within trucking circles, according to Amar Singh of Turbans 4 Australia, who works to support them.
insights.speaker: Aarti Betigeri
insights.so_what: A friction point in India-Australia migration relations that sits below the diplomatic narrative of partnership.
insights.locator: he is currently working to support Punjabi truck drivers
